quiz_questions.question_text: Tides on Earth are primarily caused by:

options.text: The gravitational pull of the Moon and the Sun

quiz_questions.explanation: Tides result from the differential gravitational pull of the Moon (primarily) and the Sun on Earth's oceans.

quiz_questions.question_text: How many high tides typically occur in a 24-hour period?
quiz_questions.explanation: Most coastal locations experience two high tides and two low tides roughly every 24 hours and 50 minutes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Spring tides occur when:

options.text: The Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned (new moon or full moon)
The Moon is at first quarter

quiz_questions.explanation: Spring tides happen at new and full moon when the Sun and Moon's gravitational pulls align, producing higher high tides.

quiz_questions.question_text: Neap tides occur when:

options.text: The Sun and Moon are at right angles relative to Earth (first or third quarter)
The Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned
Earth is closest to the Sun

quiz_questions.explanation: Neap tides occur at quarter moons when the Sun and Moon pull at right angles, partially canceling each other.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which celestial body has the strongest tidal effect on Earth?
quiz_questions.explanation: Despite being smaller, the Moon's proximity to Earth gives it about twice the tidal influence of the Sun.

quiz_questions.question_text: A tidal bulge forms on the side of Earth closest to the Moon because:

options.text: The Moon's gravity pulls water toward it more strongly on the near side
Wind blows water toward the Moon

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravitational force is stronger on the near side of Earth, pulling water into a bulge toward the Moon.

quiz_questions.question_text: There is also a tidal bulge on the side of Earth OPPOSITE the Moon because:

options.text: Earth is pulled toward the Moon more than the water on the far side, creating a relative bulge

quiz_questions.explanation: The far-side bulge forms because Earth's center is pulled toward the Moon more than the distant water, leaving it 'behind.'

quiz_questions.question_text: How much time passes between one high tide and the next?
quiz_questions.explanation: Because the Moon advances in its orbit, consecutive high tides are about 12 hours and 25 minutes apart.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tidal friction is gradually causing:

options.text: Earth's rotation to slow down and the Moon to move farther away

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal friction transfers rotational energy from Earth to the Moon, slowing Earth's spin and pushing the Moon farther away.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Bay of Fundy in Canada is known for:

options.text: Having the world's largest tidal range

quiz_questions.explanation: The Bay of Fundy has tidal ranges exceeding 16 meters due to its shape, which amplifies tidal effects through resonance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'spring tide'?

options.text: An extra-high tide that occurs when the Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned
A tide that only happens in spring

quiz_questions.explanation: Spring tides (from 'springen,' to leap) produce the greatest tidal range, occurring at new and full moons.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'neap tide'?

options.text: A moderate tide with the smallest tidal range, occurring at quarter moons
A tide during a solar eclipse
A tide with no water movement

quiz_questions.explanation: Neap tides have the smallest difference between high and low water, occurring when Sun and Moon are at right angles.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'tidal range'?

options.text: The difference in water level between high tide and low tide
The distance the tide travels inland

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal range is the vertical difference between the high water mark and the low water mark.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'tidal bulge'?

options.text: The elongation of the ocean surface toward (and away from) the Moon due to gravity

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal bulges are the raised areas of ocean water on the near and far sides of Earth relative to the Moon.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'tidal friction'?

options.text: The drag between ocean tides and the rotating Earth that gradually slows Earth's rotation
Friction between the atmosphere and space

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal friction converts Earth's rotational energy into heat, gradually slowing Earth's spin.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'tidal bore'?

options.text: A surge of water that travels upstream in a river or narrow bay during incoming tide
A deep hole caused by tides

quiz_questions.explanation: A tidal bore is a wave front that rushes up certain rivers and estuaries with the incoming tide.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'Roche limit'?

options.text: The minimum distance a moon can orbit before being torn apart by tidal forces
The maximum height of a tide
The farthest a moon can orbit

quiz_questions.explanation: The Roche limit is the distance within which a celestial body held together only by gravity would be pulled apart by tidal forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are 'tidal forces'?

options.text: The differential gravitational pull across an extended body
The total gravity of the Moon

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal forces arise because gravity varies in strength across an object — closer parts are pulled more than farther parts.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'tidal locking' in this context?

options.text: When tidal forces cause a body to always show the same face to its partner

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal locking occurs when tidal forces synchronize a body's rotation with its orbital period, as with the Moon always showing one face to Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'tidal resonance'?

options.text: When the natural period of water oscillation in a basin matches the tidal period, amplifying tides

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal resonance occurs in bays like the Bay of Fundy where the basin's natural oscillation period enhances tidal amplitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sailor notices extremely high tides during a full moon. What type of tide is this?

options.text: Spring tide — the Sun and Moon are aligned, combining their gravitational pulls

quiz_questions.explanation: At full moon, the Sun, Earth, and Moon are aligned, producing spring tides with the largest tidal range.

quiz_questions.question_text: A week after a full moon, the tidal range noticeably decreases. Why?

options.text: The Moon is at third quarter — Sun and Moon are at right angles, producing neap tides
The Moon moved farther from Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: At quarter phases, the Sun and Moon's gravitational effects partially cancel, reducing tidal range (neap tides).

quiz_questions.question_text: If the Moon were twice as far from Earth, how would tides be affected?

options.text: Tides would be much weaker since tidal force decreases with the cube of distance
Tides would be twice as strong

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal force varies inversely with the cube of distance (1/r³), so doubling distance would reduce tidal force by a factor of 8.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ancient Romans noticed tides were connected to the Moon but couldn't explain why. Newton later provided the explanation. What was it?

options.text: His law of universal gravitation explained how differential gravity creates tidal bulges
He discovered the Moon was made of water
He observed tides on the Moon

quiz_questions.explanation: Newton's gravitational theory showed that the Moon's differential gravitational pull across Earth creates the tidal bulges.

quiz_questions.question_text: Jupiter's moon Io is the most volcanically active body in the solar system. Tidal forces from Jupiter are the cause. Why is Io so affected?

options.text: Io's orbit and Jupiter's immense gravity create enormous tidal flexing that heats Io's interior
Io has a very thick atmosphere
Io has a very large iron core

quiz_questions.explanation: Jupiter's massive gravity, combined with orbital resonances with other moons, flexes Io's interior, generating extreme heat and volcanism.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coastal town builds a tidal energy power station. During which phase of the Moon would it generate the most power?

options.text: During spring tides (new or full moon) when tidal range is greatest

quiz_questions.explanation: Greater tidal range during spring tides moves more water through turbines, generating more electricity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Earth's day was much shorter billions of years ago (about 6 hours). What lengthened it?

options.text: Tidal friction from the Moon gradually slowed Earth's rotation

quiz_questions.explanation: Tidal friction has been transferring Earth's rotational energy to the Moon for billions of years, gradually lengthening the day.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student wonders why there are two tidal bulges (near side and far side) instead of just one on the side closest to the Moon. How would you explain?

options.text: On the far side, the ocean is left behind as Earth is pulled toward the Moon, creating a second bulge
The Sun creates the second bulge
Earth's spin throws water outward on the far side
One bulge is from high tide and one from low tide

quiz_questions.explanation: Differential gravity means the near side is pulled more than Earth's center, and Earth's center is pulled more than the far-side water, creating bulges on both sides.

quiz_questions.question_text: If a moon orbits within its planet's Roche limit, what would happen to it?

options.text: Tidal forces would tear it apart, potentially forming a ring system
It would crash into the planet immediately
Nothing — the Roche limit has no real effect

quiz_questions.explanation: Inside the Roche limit, tidal forces exceed the moon's self-gravity, and it would break apart — as likely happened to form Saturn's rings.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two fishing villages are on the same coastline but 200 km apart. One has a 10-meter tidal range while the other has only 2 meters. What explains this?

options.text: Local geography (bay shape, depth, and coastline) can amplify or reduce tides through resonance and funneling
One village is closer to the Moon
One is at a higher latitude

quiz_questions.explanation: Coastal features like narrow bays, underwater topography, and resonance effects dramatically influence local tidal range.
